Plasma-assisted combustion of low calorific gases under different 
oxygen-enrichment level
Ernest Bykov a,*, Simon Tixier b
, Rolandas Paulauskas a
, Adolfas Janˇcauskas a
,  
Nerijus Stri¯ugas a
a Laboratory of Combustion Processes, Lithuanian Energy Institute, Breslaujos str. 3, LT-44403 Kaunas, Lithuania
b Universit´e d’Orl´eans, Chˆateau de la Source, Avenue du Parc Floral 159, 45067 Orl´eans, France
A R T I C L E  I N F O
Keywords:
Oxygen enrichment
Plasma assisted combustion
Emissions
Near-infrared spectroscopy
Low calorific gas
A B S T R A C T
This study explores the impact of oxygen enrichment level (23, 25, and 29 % O2 in air) on plasma-assisted 
combustion of low-calorific gases (20–40 % CH4 in CO2) at various fuel-to-air ratios. Plasma parameters were 
fixed (120 kHz, 4.66 kV). Results indicate that oxygen enrichment combined with plasma-assisted combustion 
affects the process differently. High oxygen enrichment level enables stable combustion of very low-calorific 
mixtures without plasma, which was impossible otherwise. In addition, using the SPECAIR software, the influ­
ence of plasma discharges on electronic, rotational, vibrational, and translational temperatures was determined. 
Since plasma alone could not fully combust mixtures of 20 vol% CH4 in CO2, oxygen enrichment was applied for 
co-enhancement via a temperature-raising mechanism. For mixtures with 25–35 % CH4 in CO2, CO concentra­
tions in flue gases dropped by 86–90 % due to higher combustion temperatures, while NOx emissions rose by 
~93 % and ~87 %. Linking infrared emissions with CO and NOx emissions could potentially provide a better 
understanding of the optimal regimes of the low-calorific gas combustion process.
1. Introduction
Through the operational time of the landfill, the methane content in 
the emitted gas trends to decrease [1]. As landfill gas degrades to 35–40 
% methane, it is mainly co-combusted in high-temperature flares [2,3], 
for emission control, until the methane content in the gas mixture would 
not decrease to 20–25 % [4], after which those gases could not be easily 
utilised. Currently, low-calorific gases from landfills have limited pro­
cessing and utilization options for value-added products [5]. Even 
though low-calorific gases are a versatile alternative to fossil fuels with 
various practical applications [5]. However, their use in heat and power 
generation faces significant challenges. Mainly, CO2 dilution lowers the 
flame temperature and narrows the stability range [6,7]. High vari­
ability in hydrocarbon content and the presence of ballast gases, like 
CO2, severely affect fuel properties and combustion characteristics. Key 
parameters such as heating value [7,8], flame and self-ignition tem­
peratures [8,9], ignition delay [10], laminar burning velocity [11], 
flammability limits [12], combustion stability [13–15] and even com­
bustion kinetics [16,17] are altered compared to conventional fuels. 
Furthermore, changes in blend composition can cause ignition 
difficulties [18], acoustic-based combustion instabilities [19], high 
emissions, and reduced efficiency [20]. These issues highlight the need 
for effective solutions to optimize low-calorific gas combustion.
Several approaches have been proposed to improve the combustion 
temperature, stability, and heating value of low-calorific gases. These 
include optimizing fuel nozzle design, applying swirling techniques 
[21], and adjusting fuel and air velocities [22]. Chemically, combustion 
enhancement often involves adding small amounts of highly reactive 
fuels like hydrogen or strong oxidizers such as oxygen. Studies show that 
even minor H2 additions can markedly enhance flame stability [23,24], 
increase flame temperature, and improve heating value [26,27]. How­
ever, this also tends to raise NOx emissions, particularly under low to 
moderate strain rates [25]. Another promising method is MILD (Mod­
erate and Intensive Low Oxygen Dilution) combustion, aimed at 
reducing fuel consumption and addressing flame instability. Research on 
low-calorific gas flames in MILD conditions highlights that supple­
menting O2 and H2 and preheating the air effectively increases heat 
release and promotes NO formation [26,27].
The aforementioned methods for improving low-calorific gas com­
bustion are effective, but have practical and environmental drawbacks. 
H2 enrichment, for instance, can significantly reduce power output, pose

storage challenges, and lack extensive distribution infrastructure, 
coupled with real and perceived safety concerns. Hydrogen is also more 
costly per unit of energy compared to common fossil fuels and is less 
widely available [28]. In some operating and design scenarios, H2 
enrichment offers no significant benefits and can be detrimental [28]. 
Regarding MILD technology, combustion systems often cannot operate 
in flameless mode until the combustor reaches the autoignition tem­
perature of the air/fuel mixture. Otherwise, the flames could become 
unstable, increasing the risk of incidents [29].
Oxygen enrichment is a promising method to improve low-calorific 
gas combustion by using an oxidant with a higher O2 content than air 
[30]. It raises combustion temperatures, enhances thermal efficiency, 
improves ignition, stabilizes flames, and increases heat release while 
reducing exhaust gas volume and equipment costs [30]. This process 
also lowers fuel consumption per production unit [32], enabling more 
efficient use of low-calorific gases. Studies on premixed and diffusion 
flames confirm their potential for advancing energy efficiency and sus­
tainability. For example, Wang et al. [31] proposed enriching low- 
calorific gas flames with propane and oxygen, reporting significant 
flame structure changes and increased free radical production. Mean­
while, Li et al. [32] employed GriMech 3.0 to analyze low-calorific gas 
diffusion flames under varying oxygen enrichment levels, showing that 
O2 enrichment boosts net heat release and promotes free radical for­
mation. Yilmaz et al. [33] investigated premixed low-calorific gas flames 
in a pilot-scale burner, finding that flame stability improves up to 24 % 
O2 but deteriorates beyond 31 % O2, with peak NOx emissions at 28 % 
O2 [36]. According to [34], this could be explained by the NO formation 
pathways shift with methane content. In blends containing ≤12 % 
methane, the NNH mechanism dominates, while at higher methane 
levels, the prompt (Fenimore) pathway becomes more significant. 
Increased oxygen enrichment further drives thermal NOx formation via 
the Zeldovich mechanism due to elevated flame temperatures and pro­
motes the N2O route (though with less impact than the thermal 
pathway), while reducing prompt and NNH contributions irrespective of 
methane concentration. Improving combustion of low-calorific gases 
without increasing methane content is appealing due to the high costs 
associated with upgrading such gases [33,34,38]. Another technology 
that can complement oxygen enrichment for low-calorific gas combus­
tion is plasma-assisted combustion (PAC). Non-thermal plasma (NTP) 
has a wide range of applications, from medical uses like intensifying 
healing rates for various injuries [35] to material surface treatments, 
providing anti-bacterial and self-cleaning properties to polyester/cellu­
lose fabrics [36], CO2 conversion [37], and environmental control of 
harmful substances [38]. In the past d

2. Materials and methods
2.1. Experimental stand and burner
The experimental stand includes: a plasma-assisted low swirl burner, 
a high voltage pulse generator, mass flow controllers, the UV–VIS and 
NIR spectrometers, the flame chemiluminescence capturing and flow gas 
analysis systems, which are schematically shown in Fig. 1.
The plasma-assisted low-swirl burner contains a cylindrical outer 
body, made from stainless steel type 321, which serves as the ground 
electrode. It has an external diameter of Ø = 28, the part length is 44 
mm, the diameter of the inner hole is Ø = 22 mm (see Fig. 2). The inner 
electrode, shaped in the form of a cut cone serves as an inner electrode, 
connected to a high-voltage line. Cone body diameter (in widest part) is 
Ø = 20 mm, height – 38 mm and the cut cone top surface diameter 
Ø=10 mm. High voltage electrode as well is made from steel type 321.
2.2. Experimental procedure
To achieve optimal gas mixing rates, a system with a mixing chamber 
was utilized. After the mixing process, gases were directed to two 
radially positioned inlet nozzles, each with an internal diameter of 6 
mm, ensuring a stabilized velocity field for uniform gas flow. After 
reaching the swirler, the flow traversed the burner’s internal cavity, 
converging at the narrowest gap where the gliding arc was initially 
formed. The gas flow elongated the arc along the electrodes, extending 
until extinguished or short-cut by a shorter current path. This process 
covered the exhaust channel’s cross-section with plasma arcs, gener­
ating a rotating gliding arc plasma. The experimental rig is enclosed 
with plexiglass-made shields to ensure safety while combustion experi­
ments. In the top part of the rig, the ventilation port is mounted, which is 
connected to the central suction system for flue gases evacuation from 
the inner volume of the experimental rig. The burner is covered with a 
quartz tube transparent for UV and IR wavelengths (transmittance > 90 
%) with a diameter of 130 mm and a height of 730 mm. Required gases 
for combustion (air, oxygen, methane and carbon dioxide) were sup­
plied from the cylinders, and the flow controlled using mass flow con­
trollers (Brooks SLA 5800). The parameters of the gases and combustion 
conditions are presented in Table 1. The thermal power of the burner 
was kept constant (~1.32 kW) for every investigation point with various 
fuel-to-air ratios (φ) and mixtures. Gas compositions were selected to 
simulate low-calorific value gases with 20–40 vol % CH4 in CO2, which 
combustion is instable, hardly combustible or non-combustible without 
plasma assistance or oxygen enrichment decreasing methane content in 
CO2. In the current work were used five mixtures of CH4/CO2, with the 
CH4 content of 20, 25, 30, 35 and 40 vol% in CO2. For easier identifi­
cation of different mixtures, abbreviation BGXX/YY with indexes were 
introduced: XX represent CH4 content in the mixture and YY – CO2 
content of the mixture, e.g. BG20/80 would represent the mixture con­
taining 20 vol% of the CH4 and 80 vol% of the CO2 in the mixture. The 
experiments were performed with and without plasma assistance and 
using different composition oxidizer, starting from normal air (O2/N2 – 
21vol %/79 vol%) to oxygen enriched air (O2 – 23–29 vol % in N2). For 
oxygen-enriched air condition, the oxygen concentration was increased 
from 21 vol % to 23 vol %, 25 vol % and to 29 vol % of O2 in N2 (see 
Table 1).
During the plasma-assisted combustion, gliding arc discharges were 
formed using a DC high voltage generator, made by Redline Technolo­
gies (G2000). The voltage applied to the burner was set constant at 4.66 
kV and on a permanent driving frequency of 120 kHz, with power 
consumed from the socket by the generator being 135–145 W.
2.3. Flame characterization systems
For the determination of the chemiluminescence characteristic of the 
chosen radicals in the flame was used sensitive to 200–800 nm range 
ICCD (intensified charge-coupled device) camera manufactured by 
Andor. The model iStar DH734 with the observation resolution of 1024 
× 1024 pixels, photocathode with a diameter of 18 mm and the size of 
the one pixel being equal to 13 μm was used. Equivalent background

illumination below 0.2 e-/pix/sec. The carousel filter changing device 
was equipped with OH* (310 ± 10 nm), CH* (387 ± 10 nm), and C2* 
(514 ± 10 nm) hard-coated filters that allowed to capture of the spatial 
distribution of the exact range of wavelength from the flame. For camera 
control and acquired data storage was used the ANDOR SOLIS software, 
which was set up to obtain by averaging 30 acquisitions with an expo­
sure time of 0.04 s. Python 3.6 software [53] was used for data post­
processing. Matplotlib function was used for the visualisation of the 
emission intensities distribution by averaging each horizontal line of 
pixel intensity values in the frame. More info is presented in previous 
work [54].
The optical emission spectroscopic measurements have been per­
formed with a flame emission spectrometer Flame-S-XR1-ES UV–VIS 
(Ocean Insight) with wavelength range 200–1015 nm. The detector used 
in the spectrometer is a Sony ILX511B linear silicon CCD array, with 
2048 pixels. Signal-to-noise ratio is 250:1 on full signal, dynamic range 
is 1300:1 for a single acquisition, optical resolution is ~2.0 nm FWHM. 
The integration time was settled at 0.05 sec, number of scans to average 
was set to 35.
Near-infrared spectroscopy was performed using a MIR S2050-400 
(NLIR) with a wavelength range of 2000–5000 nm. Spectral Irradiance 
Sensitivity was 130,000 counts/(ms µW), optical resolution 2.4–15 nm 
(6 cm−1) and max readout rate – 400 Hz. The exposure time was set at 
0.1 sec, and the number of scans to average was set to 30.
2.4. Flue gas analysis
A flue gas sampling probe was positioned within the chamber, 60 cm 
away from the burner exit to perform sampling. This probe was secured 
within a metal cap that covered the quartz glass along the central axis. 
The sampling probe was connected to a Testo 350XL analyser, which 
was used to measure NOx and CO emissions in the flue gas. Each sam­
pling test lasted 120 s to allow for parameter stabilization.
2.5. Plasma analysis
For the plasma analysis was used SPECAIR 3.0 software [55] which 
allowed to compute and fit the obtained spectra from the UV–VIS data to 
the calculated one. The SPECAIR code [56] provides the ability to model 
up to 37 molecular transitions, including NO*, N2*, N2+*, O2*, CN*, 
OH*, NH*, C2*, and CO*, as well as atomic lines of N, O, and C. The 
model provides accurate simulations of the absolute spectral emission 
and absorption intensities of air from 80 nm

indicates increased flammability as the stable flame was determined at 
all tested regimes with different oxygen level in the oxidizer (Fig. 3B). 
Important to note that with plasma assistance the mixture of BG20/80 
(20 vol % of CH4 in CO2) was stably combusting, even on very lean 
regimes, which was unachievable without plasma assistance.
Comparing the spatial distribution of C2* of the flames without 
plasma assistance (Fig. 3A), it could be noted that the emission in­
tensities of the C2* radical is weaker for the combustion without oxygen 
enrichment compared to the enriched one. According to Li et al. [62], a 
chemiluminescence intensity peak of C2* tends to decrease with the 
increase of CO2 dilution level. Also, other groups [63] found out that C2* 
has the potential to better characterize the temperature of the flame, 
which could mean that the radical emission intensity corresponds with 
the temperature of the combustion. Also, according to the [64], the 
maximum C2* emission intensity correlates with the maximum average 
temperature at the corresponding axial locations. C2* radicals are found 
in the regions with the highest temperatures at different axial locations. 
According to this, it could be seen that with an increasing amount of air 
in the mixture, C2* emission intensities decrease, respectively, as the 
combustion process temperatures drops. By increasing the oxygen

the temperature of combustion increase, while the shape of the flame 
remains wide and V-shaped with two visible cores of the highest C2* 
emission intensities. The diapason of the stable combustion process, 
without plasma assistance, tends to decrease with increasing fuel-to-air 
ratios, most probably due to the decreasing temperature, which corre­
sponds to the decreasing emission intensity of the C2* radical. It should 
be mentioned that the shape of the flame also changed after the plasma 
was applied. Comparing plasma-assisted combustion without oxygen 
enrichment with non-assisted combustion without oxygen enrichment, 
it could be seen that with the plasma assistance, the flame prolonged 
itself, started being much narrower, and the core of the combustion 
process shifted downwards to the exit nozzle of the burner, which could 
be considered as the lift-off height [65]. As well, it could be seen that the 
emission intensity of the C2* radicals is higher, the luminosity being 
more visible, which could represent higher temperatures in the com­
bustion process. One more important thing to note is the change of the 
flame structure – from the solid V-shape with two cores for the regimes 
without plasma assistance in Fig. 3B, it be seen that the combustion core 
is situated on the axis of the burner, forming one core. It could be seen 
that in regimes, in which the mixtures with higher methane content

text:
higher oxygen level (25–29 %) in the nitrogen flow, the distribution 
pattern of the observed chemiluminescence signals was not always 
symmetrical. Possibly, that was happening due to the non-ideal match of 
the burner and quartz glass axis. It happens due to the presence of an 
additional gap for the quartz glass thermal expansion. As it was noticed 
in the work [43], the plasma decreases the lift-off parameter of the 
combustion process, thus putting the core of the combustion closer to 
the burner exit and increasing the temperatures of the glass and its 
holder. Due to that, one side makes a slightly smaller gap than another, 
which causes this side to attach the flame to itself.
3.2. Analysis of emission spectra from flames
The flame chemiluminescence revealed the influence of CO2 in 
mixtures and flammability range extension when coupling plasma 
assistance and oxygen enrichment, and emission intensities of radicals 
increase. In order to understand deeper the effect from plasma assistance 
coupling with oxygen enrichment, the UV spectra were obtained and, in 
addition, analysed using Specair software. The emission intensity graphs 
in the UV range, from 200 to 1000 nm, with and without plasma assis­
tance under different levels of oxygen enrichment for φ = 0.83, are 
shown in Fig. 4.
The most relevant and influencing on the combustion process is OH* 
radical [66,67], which was detected at 309 nm. It showed the biggest 
reaction on plasma assistance, while oxygen enrichment was rising the 
emission intensity levels of all radicals by some amount. Comparing 
emission intensity values for OH* radical with and without plasma, for 
all enrichment stages and all studied mixtures (from BG20/80 to BG40/60), 
it could be seen that OH* emission intensity increases by 16.21–2.47 
times for 21 % of oxygen in the nitrogen; by 7.41–1.42 for 23 %, by 
3.28–2.18 for 25 % and 2.8–1.6 times for 29 % of oxygen in the air, 
respectively. Emission intensities of other radicals r

text:
content, which represents the increasing thermal effect of more oxygen 
being supplied. The change of electron temperature is the change in 
internal energy with respect to entropy, while the volume and particle 
number remain constant [81]. The increase of the electron temperature 
with the remaining rotational temperature could be used as a marker 
that the main impact in the non-equilibrium gliding arc plasma that was 
used for this work comes from the increase of the kinetic energy of the 
molecules, but not from the heating ability of the plasma [81]. This can 
also explain the behaviour, observed on the Fig. 4, where the emission 
intensities of the radical increased with applied plasma.
3.3. Emission intensities of main thermal radiation emitters from flames
To determine thermal radiation characteristics changing combustion 
conditions, the flame spectra in the infrared range from 1.8 to 5.3 μm 
were obtained (Fig. 7). Mainly, the thermal radiation is emitted by three- 
atomic gases (H2O, CO2) at different wavelengths, and the peak in­
tensities could be attributed to the flame temperature change [82,83]. 
Through the combustion process, key contributors to the radiative heat 
transfer are considered to be CO2 and H2O triatomic molecules [84], and 
their formation could be influenced by different combustion process 
parameters. The mid-infrared range reveals significant peaks of H2O 
(~1800–2000, 2500 nm), mixed peak of water vapour and carbon di­
oxide (2700–3100 nm), and distinct CO2 peaks between 4300–4500 nm 
[82]. It was determined that the influence of the oxygen enrichment 
plays a more crucial role in the emission of IR-radiating gases, compared 
to the influence of the plasma assistance.
Higher temperatures and kinetic enhancement of the combustion 
process could lead to more efficient combustion, providing deeper uti­
lisation of the CO gas, existing in the mixture after plasma assistance, 
leading to increased emissions of CO2, which was also refle

title: Plasma-assisted combustion of low calorific gases under different
text:
corresponding to the lowest registered 2.7–2.8 μm peak emission in­
tensities, being 9162, 7823 and 4978 units for φ = 0.83, φ = 0.71 and φ 
= 0.63, respectively. According to [85], the auto-ignition temperature of 
CO is 700 ◦C. Still, in general, low-calorific value mixtures were strug­
gling to heat up the combustion chamber enough for sustainable CO 
combustion without a slip. Dependence of the 2.7–2.8 μm infrared 
emission intensities from the flame temperature could be seen in the 
Fig. 8. In the figure, the green line represents the calculated adiabatic 
temperatures, and the orange triangles – measured data. The R2 pa­
rameters for the fitted line were 0.91; 0.90, and 0.88 for φ = 0.82, 0.71 
and 0.63, respectively. The parameters for the calculated trendline for 
measured data can be found in Table 3 for each access air ratio.
From the Fig. 8 it could be seen, that the temperature is trending to 
rise with rising emission intensity of the H2O + CO2 (~2.7–2.8 μm) 
peaks. That could also explain increasing Trot, which can be seen 
trending to rise on the Fig. 6, which is known to be a marker of the gas 
temperature.
3.4. Influence of oxygen enrichment on combustion process
The content of the oxidizer in the fuel–air mixture influences the 
radical generation and the plasma temperature parameter. As it could be 
seen on the Fig. 9, the mixture, which is containing 20 % of CH4 in CO2, 
was not combustible without plasma assistance. But when the highest 
rate (29 % of O2 in N2) of oxygen enrichment level was applied, the 
combustion process of BG20/80 sustained without plasma assistance. On 
the Fig. 9 could be observed the dependence of the O2 fraction in flue 
gases from three studied fuel-to-air ratios (φ = 0.83, φ = 0.71, φ = 0.63, 
respectively). It also could be noted that the difference in O2 fraction in 
flue gases for 25 % O2 in N2 and 29 % of O2 in air was varying by 3.76, 
5.6 and 13.9 % for φ = 0.83, φ = 0.71 and φ = 0.63, respectively, for 
BG25/75.

text:
with it). For 23 % O2 in N2, for BG25/75, the difference in oxygen content 
in the flue gas was 52 and 37 % for φ = 0.83 and φ = 0.71, respectively. 
For the regime with 25 % O2 in N2 the difference was 28; 48 and 38 % 
and for the regime with 29 % the difference was 46, 33 and 26 % for φ =
0.83, 0.71 and 0.63, respectively. BG30/70 showed the same trend, the 
difference between regimes with and without plasma assistance was 46 
and 38 % for the air mixture containing 21 % O2 in N2 – 37, 39 and 40 % 
for 23 % O2 in N2 – 26, 23 and 30 % for 25 % of oxygen in air and 6, 5 
and 6 % for 29 % of oxygen in air mixture for φ = 0.83, 0.71 and 0.63 
respectively. For the BG35/65, the difference in oxygen content is flue 
gases between regimes with and without plasma assistance was 44 and 
21 % for the air mixture containing 21 % of oxygen in air, 57, 24 and 28 
% for 23 % oxygen in air, 4, 5 and 6 % for 25 % of oxygen in air for φ =
0.83, 0.71 and 0.63 respectively. BG40/60 showed the smallest difference 
between assisted and non-assisted regimes. Difference was 16, 18 and 
25 % for the air mixture containing 21 % O2 in N2 and 8, 11 and 14 % for 
23 % O2 in N2 for φ = 0.83, 0.71 and 0.63 respectively. On mixtures with 
higher oxygen content in air burner was quickly overheating, making it 
impossible to obtain the data.
3.5. Combustion products at different oxygen enrichment levels with and 
without plasma assistance
A combination of plasma assistance with oxygen enrichment [93,94] 
leads to more efficient combustion of CO, which is partially formed from 
CO2 cracking by the plasma. At the same time, higher temperatures due 
to a higher level of O2 in the oxidizer could lead to increased generation 
of NOx emission through the thermal mechanism of their generation. As 
it is shown in the literature, the thermal (Zeldovich) mechanism could 
be named dominant for the flame temperature being higher 1800 K [86]. 
On the Fig. 10 could be observed graphs showing the dependence of

0.63) temperature in combustion chamber was not able to reach con­
ditions for sustainable combustion of CO gas (see Fig. 8). For BG25/75, 
captured emissions were guided by the same trend: for the lowest 
enrichment the emissions even were increasing, from 3952 to 7027 ppm, 
for regimes without and with plasma, respectively, due to the impact of a 
plasma processing CO2 gas into CO. After enriching the air with 23 and 
25 % of O2 in N2, was observed a tendency of CO emissions to decrease 
for the richest studied conditions (φ = 0.83), resulting in a change from 
4957 to 3520 ppm (by 28.9 %) for the 23 % enrichment regime and from 
3430 to 1205 ppm (by 64.87 %) for 25 % enrichment regime. BG25/75 
under 25 % of enrichment already had enough thermal potential to 
combust CO even with leaner conditions, with φ = 0.71, when CO 
emissions decreased from 3695 to 2367 (by 35.94 %) comparing re­
gimes without and with plasma assistance, respectively. Higher oxygen 
enrichment (29 % of O2 in N2) results in higher combustion tempera­
tures, which leads to better and more efficient CO combustion. For 
further stated enrichment regime emissions dropped from 2243 to 218 
(by 90.2 %), from 2643 to 216 (by 91.8 %) and from 3104 to 420 (by 
86.46 %) ppm for φ = 0.83, 0.71 and 0.63, respectively, when 
comparing regimes without and with plasma assistance. It should be 
noted that such low CO emissions could mean full CO combustion in the 
combustion chamber and the most efficient fuel utilization that has been 
achieved with the current research setup and mixtures for the gas con­
taining 25 % of CH4 in the CO2 stream. Mixture with the 30 % of CH4 in 
the CO2 stream was acting the same: showing no decrease in CO emis­
sions for non-enriched conditions, 23 % of O2 in the air was enough to 
decrease CO emission levels from 3936 to 990 (by 74.84 %) and from 
4038 to 1712 (by 57.6 %) ppm for φ = 0.83, 0.71, respectively, 
comparing regime without and with plasma assistance. 25 and 29 %

text:
desirable emissions such as NOx. Fig. 10 shows graphs of NOx emission, 
which was made by adding NO emissions to NO2 emissions. The tem­
perature impact on NOx generation was high, resulting in emission in­
crease by 73–88 % (from 40 to 77 to about 340 ppm) for BG20/80, when 
comparing regimes with plasma and without plasma. While the per­
centage level of the nitrogen oxides increased for other mixtures (by 
77–92 % for BG25/75, by 88–98 % for BG30/70, by 78–94 % for BG35/65, 
and by 49–90 % for BG40/60), in absolute numbers, the levels of NOx 
emission increase were not that drastic. Without plasma assistance, for 
non-enriched regimes, NOx emissions were varying between 11 and 67 
ppm (minimal and maximal spotted values for all tested mixtures). The 
same behaviour was observed by the other authors, specifying in their 
work [87], that the NO emissions reduced with the increasing amounts 
of carbon dioxide introduced into the system, while being under reduced 
oxygen influence. With plasma assistance, for the same 21 % of oxygen 
in the air, they were 125–392 ppm (minimal and maximal spotted values 
for all tested mixtures). Low-enriched non-assisted combustion (with 23 
% of O2 in N2) emitted 8–44 ppm of NOx, and assisted combustion 
emitted 166–386 ppm of NOx. 25 % of oxygen in the air, without 
assistance, resulted in 7–85 ppm, and the combustion with the same 
enrichment in the assisted regime generated 174–460 ppm of NOx. For 
the highest tested enrichment with 29 % of O2 in air, the combustion 
generated 11–77 ppm in the non-assisted regime and 176–493 ppm for 
assisted.
3.6. Correlation of flue gas emissions and infrared emission intensities
Spontaneous transitions between distinct molecular energy states 
allow infrared-active gases to absorb or emit radiation. Each molecular 
structure could emit or absorb these photons at particular wavelengths. 
The identity of a molecular species can be established by analyzing the 
intensity of the intense characteri

At the same time, combining the data from the Figs. 8 and 11, it could

relatively low, being 2–3 ⋅ 103 ppm, tending to increase with increasing

could be explained by the mechanism described in part 3.3, where the 
combustion process produces enough energy for stable combustion, and

its potential for the combustion process support. At the same time, while 
measured temperature reaches 880, 813 and 716 ◦C for φ = 0.83, φ =

additional assembly heating, which results in increased carbon mon­
oxide slip. That also corresponds with the emission of NOx and NO, 
being lower at the 10 103 counts area, when compared to the area with 
lower infrared emission intensities (and lower temperatures, which, 
respectively, means combustion occurring with lower calorific value 
gas). After that, in the 12.5–20 103 counts region, a rapid decrease of the 
CO emissions in the flue gases and, respectively, a rapid increase in the 
NO/NOx production. While the left slope of a CO peak was formed 
mainly by the data obtained from the low-calorific value fuel, the proper 
slope shows the presence of higher calorific value gases, e.g., BG35/65 
and BG40/60. The concentration of points from these mixtures tends to 
increase with increasing infrared emission intensities. This region of 
infrared intensities corresponds with temperatures in the range of 
850–1000 ◦C (for various fuel-to-air ratios, enrichment regimes, and 
mixtures, respectively).

Warming Potential being negative 11 in this perspective. At the same 
time, CO has a more moderate but stable Global Warming Potential of 
2–3.3 from a 100-year perspective, which makes it a less desirable 
emitter due to its positive long-term impact. By superimposing the CO 
graph on the NOx one, the point of their intersection can be obtained, 
which can be considered as the point showing the optimal operation 
region. This point appears to be in the 14–16 103 counts region, but due 
to the discussed behaviour of CO and NOx, it should be moved to the

of the combustion process equal to 940 ◦C and mixtures BG30/70 and
